does: This paper introduces SafeSteer, a decoding-level defense mechanism for multimodal large language models that operates during token generation without requiring model fine-tuning. The method comprises two main components: a Decoding-Probe that detects harmful outputs during generation and iteratively steers the decoding process toward safety by adjusting token probabilities, and a modal semantic alignment vector that transfers textual safety alignment knowledge to the vision modality. The authors first establish that MLLMs possess intrinsic safety capabilities by showing models can distinguish harmful from harmless inputs during decoding, and they observe that image-based attacks are more stealthy than text-based ones. Experiments demonstrate that SafeSteer effectively defends against both text and image-based jailbreak attacks while maintaining model performance and generalizability to novel attacks.
connects: This work builds on recent research in jailbreak defenses for large language models and extends it to the multimodal setting. It responds to limitations of existing defense approaches—costly fine-tuning methods like safety-specific instruction tuning and inefficient post-hoc interventions such as output filtering—by leveraging the observation that models contain inherent safety knowledge. The paper draws on decoding-level intervention research that manipulates token distributions during generation, and connects to cross-modal alignment work showing how knowledge transfers between modalities. It also relates to studies on adversarial robustness in vision-language models and the particular vulnerabilities of multimodal systems to image-based attacks that exploit the weaker alignment of visual safety compared to textual safety.
enables: SafeSteer opens pathways for efficient, generalizable defense against evolving jailbreak attacks in multimodal systems. The decoding-level approach could inspire similar lightweight interventions for other safety-critical applications beyond jailbreak defense, such as steering models away from other harmful behaviors during generation. The modal semantic alignment vector mechanism suggests broader possibilities for transferring safety properties between modalities and modalities in other multimodal architectures. The demonstrated ability to defend against unseen attacks without retraining points toward building more robust foundation models with transferable safety properties. Future work could explore how this defense mechanism scales to larger models, combines with other safety techniques, or extends to other modality combinations beyond vision and language.
